issues.title: No Gantt chart component: schedule page renders flat list instead of interactive timeline
issues.description: The Schedule page lacks a proper Gantt chart visualization. Construction PMs need horizontal timeline bars with dependency arrows, critical path highlighting, baseline vs actual comparison, and drag to reschedule. Currently the page shows a basic table or card layout with no timeline component. This is the single biggest gap blocking adoption — superintendents check their Gantt daily.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The page renders schedule activities as a flat table or card list with no timeline visualization, no horizontal bars, no dependency arrows, and no critical path highlighting. AFTER: Replace the main content area with a construction-grade Gantt chart. Import and use a new GanttChart component from '../components/schedule/GanttChart'. The page should show: (1) KPI cards at top — Schedule Variance (days), Critical Path Items count, On Track %, Complete %. Calculate these from the schedule data: variance = count of activities where actual_finish > end_date or status === 'delayed', critical path count = activities where is_critical_path === true, on track = activities not delayed / total * 100, complete = activities where status === 'completed' / total * 100. (2) View toggle buttons for 'Gantt' and 'List' views. (3) The Gantt view renders each activity as a horizontal bar positioned by start_date and end_date on a time axis. Critical path activities get a red left border (3px solid #E74C3C). Completed activities are gray (#9CA3AF). In progress are blue (#3B82F6). Delayed are amber (#F5A623). Not started are light gray (#E5E7EB). Each bar shows the activity name left-aligned inside. Percent complete shown as a darker fill within the bar. (4) Baseline bars shown as thin gray bars (4px height) above the actual bars when baseline_start and baseline_finish exist. (5) Milestone activities (where is_milestone is true or duration is 0) render as diamond shapes instead of bars. (6) Float days shown on hover tooltip. (7) A zoom control with Day/Week/Month buttons that adjusts the time scale. (8) Empty state: 'Build your schedule to track every phase from mobilization to closeout' with CTA 'Import from P6/MS Project' and 'Create First Phase'. Use inline styles with theme tokens from '../styles/theme'. Use framer-motion for bar entrance animations (staggerChildren: 0.03). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No schedule import capability: users cannot upload P6 XER or MS Project files
issues.description: The PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA specifies 'Import from P6/MS Project' as a required empty state CTA and the PRODUCTION_ROADMAP lists P0-2 Schedule Import as a dealbreaker. Currently there is no file upload UI, no parsing logic, and no way to populate the schedule from industry standard formats. Without this, every superintendent keeps P6 open in a separate tab.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: There is no schedule import functionality. No file upload button, no drag-drop zone, no import modal. Users have no way to bring their existing P6 or MS Project schedule into SiteSync. AFTER: Add an 'Import Schedule' button in the page header next to any existing action buttons. Style it as a secondary button (white background, 1px gray border, dark text, border-radius 8px, height 40px). When clicked, open a modal dialog with: (1) A drag-and-drop zone (dashed border, 200px height, light gray background) that accepts .xer, .xml, .mpp, and .csv files. Text inside: 'Drag your schedule file here or click to browse'. Supported formats listed below: 'Primavera P6 (.xer, .xml), Microsoft Project (.mpp), CSV (.csv)'. (2) A file input triggered by clicking the drop zone. (3) On file selection, show the filename, file size, and a progress bar. (4) A 'Preview' step that shows the first 10 activities parsed from the file in a simple table (Name, Start, End, Duration, Status). For the MVP, parse CSV files client-side by splitting on commas and mapping columns. For XER/XML/MPP, show a message 'Processing... This file will be sent to our import service' and call supabase.functions.invoke('schedule-import', { body: formData }) where formData contains the file. (5) A 'Confirm Import' button that inserts parsed activities into schedule_phases via supabase. (6) Success toast: 'Imported X activities from [filename]'. (7) Error handling: if the file format is unrecognized, show 'Unsupported file format. Please use .xer, .xml, .mpp, or .csv'. Use the modal pattern from existing codebase (Card component with overlay). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Schedule page has zero ARIA attributes, no keyboard navigation, no screen reader support
issues.description: The Global Acceptance Standards require all pages pass axe-core with zero critical/serious violations. The schedule page has no role attributes on the timeline, no aria-labels on interactive elements, no keyboard navigation for activities, no focus management, and no aria-live regions for status updates. This fails WCAG 2.1 AA.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The schedule page has no ARIA attributes. Interactive elements like activity rows/bars lack role, aria-label, and tabIndex attributes. Status changes are not announced. Keyboard users cannot navigate between activities. AFTER: (1) Add role='main' and aria-label='Project Schedule' to the outermost page container div. (2) On every KPI metric card, add aria-label that includes the label and value, e.g. aria-label='Schedule Variance: 3 days behind'. (3) On the activity list/table, add role='grid' with aria-label='Schedule activities'. Each row gets role='row' and tabIndex={0}. Each cell gets role='gridcell'. (4) Add aria-label to each activity row: aria-label={`${activity.name}, ${activity.status}, ${activity.percent_complete}% complete, starts ${activity.start_date}`}. (5) Add keyboard handlers: onKeyDown on each row — Enter or Space opens detail, ArrowDown moves to next row, ArrowUp moves to previous row. Implement with document focus management using refs or data-index attributes. (6) On status filter tabs/buttons, add role='tablist' to the container and role='tab' with aria-selected to each tab. (7) Add an aria-live='polite' region that announces when the activity list updates, e.g. 'Showing 45 of 120 activities' or 'Schedule updated'. (8) On any loading skeleton, add aria-label='Loading schedule data' and role='status'. (9) On the empty state, add role='status' and aria-label with the empty state message text. (10) Ensure all icon-only buttons have aria-label (zoom in, zoom out, filter, import). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Schedule page not usable on mobile: no responsive layout, no touch targets, Gantt not adapted
issues.description: PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA section 6 item 12 requires: 'Mobile: Gantt replaced with a timeline list view (vertical). Each phase is a card with start/end dates, progress bar, and status. Tap to expand details.' Currently the page does not adapt to mobile widths. KPI cards overflow, tables break at 375px, and touch targets are smaller than 44px.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The schedule page uses fixed-width layouts that overflow on mobile. KPI cards render in a 4-column grid that breaks below 768px. Activity rows have small touch targets. No vertical timeline view exists for mobile. AFTER: (1) Wrap the KPI cards grid in a container that uses CSS grid with gridTemplateColumns: 'repeat(auto-fit, minmax(160px, 1fr))' so cards stack on narrow screens. On screens below 768px (detect with a useMediaQuery hook or window.innerWidth check via useState/useEffect), show cards in a 2-column grid. Below 480px, show single column. (2) Below 768px, replace the Gantt/table view with a vertical timeline card layout. Each activity renders as a Card component (white background, 12px border-radius, 1px border #E5E7EB, padding 16px, marginBottom 12px) showing: activity name (16px, 600 weight), start and end dates (14px, muted #6B7280), a horizontal progress bar (8px height, green fill proportional to percent_complete, gray track, border-radius 4px), status badge (8px colored dot + text), and float days in small muted text. (3) Each card has minHeight 64px and the entire card is tappable (cursor pointer, onClick to expand/navigate to detail). (4) All touch targets are minimum 44x44px. Buttons and interactive elements have padding to meet this minimum. (5) Add a sticky header on mobile with the page title and filter button. (6) Critical path activities on mobile show a red left border (3px solid #E74C3C) on the card. (7) Milestone activities show a diamond icon (◆) next to the name instead of a progress bar. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Schedule uses mock data instead of Supabase queries with proper loading and empty states
issues.description: FOUNDER PRIORITIES P0 mandates replacing ALL mock data with real Supabase hooks. The schedule page either uses hardcoded mock arrays or imports from a mock data file. When the database is empty, it should show a helpful empty state guiding users to create their first phase or import a schedule, not a blank table. Loading state should show skeleton loaders matching the Gantt/list layout.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Schedule.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The page imports or defines mock schedule data (hardcoded arrays of activities, or imports from a mock file). Loading states are absent or show a generic spinner. When no data exists, the page shows a blank area or generic 'No data found' text. AFTER: (1) Remove all mock data imports and hardcoded activity arrays. (2) Use the supabase client to fetch real data: import { supabase } from '../lib/supabase'. Create a useEffect that fetches from schedule_phases (or schedule_activities if that table exists): const { data, error } = await supabase.from('schedule_phases').select('*').eq('project_id', currentProjectId).order('start_date', { ascending: true }). Store in useState. (3) Get currentProjectId from URL params, context, or a project store. If no project ID is available, show 'Select a project to view its schedule'. (4) Add a loading state with isLoading boolean. While loading, render skeleton loaders: 4 skeleton metric cards (each 120px height, pulsing gray rectangles with border-radius 12px) in a row, then 8 skeleton activity rows (each 48px height, full width, with alternating slightly different widths to mimic activity bars). Use CSS animation: background linear-gradient shimmer, animationDuration '1.5s', animationIterationCount 'infinite'. (5) When data loads but is empty (data.length === 0), show the designed empty state: a calendar/Gantt icon (use CalendarDays from lucide-react, size 48, color #9CA3AF), heading 'Build your schedule to track every phase from mobilization to closeout' (18px, 600 weight, #1A1A2E), subtext 'Import your P6 or MS Project schedule, or create phases manually' (14px, 400, #6B7280), two CTA buttons: primary 'Import Schedule' (orange background #F47820, white text) and secondary 'Create First Phase' (white background, gray border). Center this vertically and horizontally. (6) Add a real-time subscription for schedule_phases changes so updates from other users appear live. (7) Add error handling: if the fetch fails, show a toast or inline error 'Unable to load schedule' with a 'Retry' button. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

invented_features.title: Weather Overlay on Schedule Timeline
invented_features.description: Overlay 7-day weather forecast icons directly on the Gantt chart timeline header. Activities flagged as outdoor_activity that coincide with rain or extreme weather days get an amber warning badge. Clicking the weather icon shows: 'Rain forecasted Thursday — outdoor concrete pour at risk. Consider rescheduling to Monday.' This is the killer feature no competitor has: schedule intelligence that accounts for Mother Nature.

invented_features.title: Three Week Lookahead Generator
invented_features.description: One-click button that generates a filtered view showing only activities within the next 21 calendar days, sorted by start date, grouped by trade/responsible party. Exportable as a PDF formatted for weekly subcontractor coordination meetings. This is the most-used schedule view on every construction site and currently requires manual filtering in P6.

competitive_intel: Procore's scheduling module is basic — no native Gantt, relies on third-party integrations (Oracle P6, MS Project). Fieldwire has zero schedule capabilities. Buildertrend has a decent Gantt for residential but lacks critical path analysis and P6 import. Autodesk Construction Cloud has strong BIM-to-schedule 4D linking but weak field UX. SiteSync's opportunity: be the first platform where a superintendent can see their P6 schedule, overlay weather risk, and get AI delay predictions without leaving the app. The schedule import (P0-2) and Gantt visualization are the minimum bar to even demo to a GC. Currently SiteSync scores below every competitor on scheduling because it has no visual timeline at all.
summary: The Scheduling module is critically underdeveloped at 19/100 — the lowest-scoring module in the system. It lacks the fundamental Gantt chart visualization that every construction PM expects, has no schedule import capability (P0-2 dealbreaker from PRODUCTION_ROADMAP), renders no real data from Supabase, has zero accessibility support, and is completely unusable on mobile. The five issues target the most impactful gaps: building the Gantt chart component with critical path highlighting and baseline comparison (domain_depth), adding P6/MS Project import (interaction_quality), implementing ARIA attributes and keyboard navigation (accessibility), creating a responsive mobile timeline view (mobile_first), and wiring real Supabase data with proper loading/empty states (data_richness). These five fixes would lift the score from 19 to approximately 50-55 and make the schedule page demoable to GCs.
